_comment: Voice-ID guide content, distilled from docs/wiki/members/*.md (throwaway93257 Reddit archive). Keyed by group slug -> member id (matching songs/<slug>/group.json). Rendered by src/guide.ts on guide.html. 't' values use the player's deep-link format (Mm SS, e.g. 1m15); songId is resolved against songs/index.anime.json at render time, so unknown ids degrade to plain text.

1.thesis: The leader who sings like one — warm, endlessly consistent, defined by “comfiness”: a softness so gentle it stays soothing even on sad songs.
tells: Signature warmth — a rich, soft, “comfy” tone, reassuring even on sad tracks.
Master of breath control: adds and reels in power “on a dime” with smooth breathiness.
A surprise chorus falsetto, plus near-whisper soft passages.
Tends to fade pleasantly into the background through sheer consistency.

signatureSongs.label: The Honoka microcosm — slow passionate intro flowing into bubbly pop; best for sensing her range.
Her “godlike” bridge solo.

notes.text: Provides her own angelic backup vocals in the pre-chorus; says “Snow Halation” at 1:01.
A more aggressive interpretation than Hanayo's — power persists through the chorus to the climactic moment.
Soft soothing pre-choruses that drop to a near-whisper on “sekai”.
Flutters “mare”/“kero” for added complexity; her solo could pass for a solo single.
Handles the requisite rock intensity — she's far more capable than her jazz reputation suggests.

2.thesis: The “vibrato queen” — an angelic, mature soprano whose defining trait is being soft and dramatic at once; her Natsu, Owaranai de solo is repeatedly called the best in the franchise.
tells: Uses vibrato in literally everything — more frequent and prominent than Umi's.
A noticeably nasal pronunciation of “ah” sounds.
Soft intros that burst into powerful choruses, often starting deeper to carry sadness.
A mature, angelic tone — innocent without Hanayo's childlike quality.
confusable: vs Umi: in shared songs the deeper sections are Umi; Eli is more nasal and adds more vibrato (e.g. on “de” at 0:03 of Aishiteru Banzai! where Umi doesn't).

signatureSongs.label: “The Eli Show” — a ~40-second solo repeatedly named the best solo in the whole franchise.
Her duet with Nozomi — signature vibrato + warmth selling love and longing.

notes.text: Soft intro into a powerful chorus burst, starting deeper for sadness (an Amy Lee / “My Immortal” comparison).
Powerhouse soprano against Umi's alto — all the higher sections are hers.
Embodies elegance in verse 2 of the famed Umi/Eli/Maki trio.
Soft and delicate, but also sensual and mature.

3.thesis: The fluffy, high-pitched cutie whose real strength isn't her lead voice — it's being the group's cleverest, most foundational background harmonizer.
tells: A high, “fluffy,” cutesy voice with a distinctive sharpness.
The group's best background/harmony singer — sets the foundation, normally “hard to locate” in a mix.
Can drop into a deeper register without losing the cute factor (sounds Maki-esque at her deepest).
Surprising power bursts and clean control: stays breathy, ends notes low, then alternates high/low.

signatureSongs.label: Her pre-anime solo — deeper, with conviction and an aggressive edge; this chorus is a favorite moment.
A Kotori-center single — her harmonies and falsettos lead into each verse.

notes.text: Best example of her pitch control — stays breathy, ends notes low, alternates high/low.
Her deepest range, sounding Maki-esque.
Bigger than usual here — choruses normally inaudible from her come through crystal clear.
Crisp ascending scales without straining.

4.thesis: The technical beast of μ's — a jack-of-all-trades alto whose defining trait is sheer range: she runs the full gamut of power/pitch combinations better than anyone.
tells: An alto powerhouse with deep, long vowels who dominates group sections.
Emphatic, well-placed vibrato; sweeps notes instead of hard breaks.
Precise pitch micro-adjustments on nearly every note; soft-to-“opera-house” in 15 seconds.
So consistent she rarely deviates from her norm — which is itself a tell.
confusable: vs Eli: the deeper sections are Umi; she's less nasal on “ah” and uses less, less-prominent vibrato.

signatureSongs.label: Her 2nd solo verse goes from soft and steady to “filling an entire opera house” in 15 seconds.
Explosive “booming Umi vocals” — power stays sky-high; “feels like it should be a solo.”

notes.text: High pitch + vibrato on “kanjitanda” — “listen until 0:07 and you'll be convinced.”
Goes all-in on power in verse 2; dominates group sections.
Sweeps “go-oru” instead of a hard break — hallmark technique.
Fantastic alto against Eli's soprano — their only studio duet.

5.thesis: The most surprising talent in μ's — an energetic, infectiously happy voice whose defining trait is clarity: a purity that cuts through any nine-member mix.
tells: Pure clarity — overpowers everyone on long vowels in group sections, audible even buried in nine voices.
Cleanest on vowels: soft O's, hard E's, hard O's, and unusually clean hard U's (where others “sink”).
Brightest on energetic, happy songs; “just does not do deep or breathy.”
More technical than her cute persona implies — vibrato arguably stronger than Umi's up high.
In character, drops cat-like “nya” interjections (Kaguya no Shiro de Odoritai 0:49; the explosive “Nyaaa!” in Dancing Stars on Me!).

signatureSongs.label: Her vibrato-and-clarity showcase — the full extent of her natural voice.
Hard U “sawagu” audible over everyone in the 9-member version.

notes.text: “Lightning to Umi's thunder” — clarity and purity, clear English “can you hear.”
Gorgeous falsetto with Umi, building to the chorus.
Reimagines the dramatic song with bouncy clarity and soft falsetto.

6.thesis: The red-haired piano girl and perennial popularity champion — a diva-grade voice defined by the timely use of power: knowing exactly when to detonate and when to hold back.
tells: A slightly raspy nasality paired with a stiff, equally-paced vibrato.
Queen of powerful, sustained high notes; never overpowered by busy instrumentals.
Sweet spot is a mid-high range with mild nasality, especially on soft O's.
Strong fit with electronic / hip-hop / K-pop-influenced pop.

signatureSongs.label: Her magnum opus — raspy/breathy with timely power; the intro is the single most-cited favorite.
Go-to for sexually-charged vocals; hangs on “zettai.”

notes.text: Occupies the soft, breathy space — her soft opening note makes Umi's entry explosive.
Best in a mid-high range over dramatic instrumentation — “bouken wa kiken…” cited as perfection.
Hits falsetto then drops pitch seamlessly into the chorus.
Builds to the climactic “Snow Halation!”

7.thesis: The most distinguishable voice in μ's yet the one with the least spotlight — a warm, maternal mezzo who is the group's “emulsifier”, blending the high and deep voices together.
tells: Warm, maternal, soothing — best in the lower registers.
The harmonizing glue: even when not distinctly audible she smooths others' voices (e.g. Eli's vibrato).
Sits between the high and low voices on pitch duties (between Rin and Umi in lily white).
A unique, slightly divisive timbre; sings more from the nose than the chest.
confusable: vs Aqours' Kanan: very similar — close pitch, both sing more from the nose than the chest, both stronger at blended/soft work than power solos.

signatureSongs.label: Stands out on her own in the chorus background — shines in fast, powerful choruses.
Her harmonizing role on full display — the post-chorus “la”s smooth out Eli's vibrato.

notes.text: “The winner” — power manipulation and the clearest evidence of her improvement.
“Nozomi just does not do vibrato, let alone this good” — bold and front-and-center.
The deepest Nozomi ever recorded.

8.thesis: The franchise's “shy girl” incarnate — a Jekyll-and-Hyde vocalist who switches on demand between a cute high voice and a deep soft one; her Love Marginal is repeatedly named the best solo in Love Live.
tells: Two voices — a distinctly cute high one and a distinctly deep soft one; her versatility is the switching between them.
Cuteness lives in rounded, exaggerated vowels (open jaw, forward tongue).
Shyness shows in subdued projection and wavering, fluttery notes.
A pop/ballad type — the songs written for her are all “pure,” no rock.

signatureSongs.label: Overwhelmingly the corpus's favorite — the deep/soft voice nailing complex heartbreak; bridge + final chorus.
Gold-standard “Low Voice Hanayo” — a rock ballad written for pre-anime her.

notes.text: The closest the later era gets to another Love Marginal — soft, then erupts with strength and vibrato.
Both voices, even simultaneously.
“hoo-ah”s and beautiful vibrato on “nogasazu ni.”

9.thesis: The “#1 idol in the universe” — not the most polished of μ's, but the most ambitious, reaching for techniques no one else attempts; and the group's rapper.
tells: Rounds off pitches and chops phrases into discrete staccato syllables, with a pouty, forward-jaw delivery.
Rotates rapidly through deliveries in one song — heady midrange, poutiness, breathiness, added/removed nasality.
Her signature is rapping — lightning-fast, bright, in-character bars.
Breath-control tell: she sometimes arrives at choruses nearly out of breath.
Slips her own name in as an in-character interjection (a “Nico” at 1:04 in Kaguya no Shiro de Odoritai).
confusable: Sounds like Nozomi in warm/soft registers, like Rin in rounded highs, and like Aqours' Ruby on rounded note-enders.

signatureSongs.label: The emergence of “Nico the rapper” — lightning-fast, bright bars; a favorite Nico moment.
A Twista-speed, in-character rap bridge that “encapsulates her character.”

notes.text: Lets loose vocally, handling power without faltering; signature jaw adjustments.
One of her strongest for clarity, power, and pitch control.
“Excellent” — an unusually wide, accurate array of techniques, one of her strongest closers.

1.thesis: Dismissed early as a Honoka copy, she became one of Aqours' most expressive singers — conveying emotion through intricate technical tricks rather than raw power.
tells: A peculiar hard O that mixes a hard “oh” with a soft “ah” (e.g. “gomen ne” in Yozora).
An emphatic hard E via a high-tongue, forward-mouth approach (“kirari,” Daisuki 1:24).
“Singing-through-tears” rough texturing, head/chest shifting, multi-variation phrase repeats.
In big open solos she opens every vowel and can sound like a different character entirely.
confusable: Can sound like Honoka in opening solos, like Ruby in soft passages, and shades of Riko/Mari in big solos.

signatureSongs.label: Career-defining — three distinct emotional takes on “kienai” via dynamics, texture, and breathing.
A tour de force — the 2:36 solo “changed the game”; she doesn't sound like the same character.

notes.text: Rough “nakisou” texturing (singing-through-tears); powerful sustained vibrato later.
Climax — soft “osoi ne,” vibrato'd “gomen”; a “gives me chills” moment.
Aggressive chorus lines; emphatic hard E on “kirari.”
Nimble dynamic control, dipping in and out of softer ranges.

2.thesis: The deep-voiced tomboy “beast” — a powerful chest voice, wildly recognizable; inconsistent, but at her best among the group's finest.
tells: Two distinct voices — a powerful, deep, tomboyish chest voice she drops into for climaxes, and a lighter everyday one.
Pitch stiffness — flat, each word its own distinct pitch, little dynamics.
The only member who does tremolo (fast vibration) on words like “daisuki”/“areba.”
A hard forward U almost like an L (Daisuki 3:59 “naru”); stands out on long “u” vowels.

signatureSongs.label: Her showcase — soft solos that explode into power; the easiest place to hear her vibrato.
Dominates the harmony via an emphasized long “u” in “naru”; “daisuki” maybe her best note ever.

notes.text: Quirk is vibrato (four instances); a deeper voice with solid dynamics; sounds tomboyish.
Deep voice not just for climaxes; noticeably stiff but stands out; strong vibrato on “happy.”
Two distinct voices; the deep powerful one used for the climax.
Stands out on an extended long “u” in group sections via unique pronunciation.

3.thesis: The crystal-clear vocal “glue” of Aqours — a no-frills, voice-quality-over-technicality style and consistent timbre make her one of the easiest members to ID.
tells: Most reliable tell: an upward-sliding pitch at the end of a line — she does it constantly.
Possibly the clearest singer in the group; mezzo pitch, strong projection — the vocal foundation.
Goes high on the ends of long notes; occasional extra-high-pitched lines.
A narrow set of cadences and note progressions — consistent “to a fault.”

signatureSongs.label: Bursts back into the chorus after a dropout — chill-inducing; shows she's the glue that blends the trio.
Deceptively expressive via cadence — drags “tsu,” asymmetric cadences, hard enunciation.

notes.text: Syncs with Yoshiko and Ruby — clarity as a solid foundation.
Unmatched clarity makes the falsetto potent.
No-frills, voice-quality-over-technicality; upward-slide note enders.
Soft deep vibrato on “ga” — buttery smoothness extending into her softer range.

4.thesis: A husky power-and-warmth powerhouse with one of the biggest voices in Aqours — top-tier latent talent the author argues has been underexposed by song writing, not ability.
tells: A naturally husky voice; depth audible even in soft sections.
Deep vowels and sudden bursts of power with no ramp (“saa saa” at Pikkapika 2:05).
Clear, open vowels in her high range.
A narrow vibrato — the key tell separating her from Yoshiko's wider one.
confusable: vs Yoshiko: Hanamaru's vibrato is narrow; Yoshiko's is wider.

signatureSongs.label: The breakout — “the first time she truly left nothing on the table”; a huge outlier on the consistency graph.
High notes higher than they have any right to be given how deep her voice is — incredible power and range.

notes.text: Sudden booming “kamo ne”; effortless power vocals with depth even in soft parts.
Crams power, technicality, and clarity into a limited frame — Play of the Game two primers running.
Strong vibrato; her huskier voice a natural fit; sets the group-section foundation.

5.thesis: The “biggest surprise” of Aqours — a moe singer who treads the right side of cute-vs-childish, with a real, maturing technical voice hiding under the kid-character shtick.
tells: Childlike characteristics; a very straightforward style with little texturing and slurred transitions.
Poor clarity on hard consonants/vowels (S in “isa” ≈ “sh” in “shiku”; an R/L/Y hybrid “ra”).
Keeps sustained notes flat where others would add vibrato.
A rounded, high-throat delivery of soft vowels that is unique to her.
confusable: Soft, rounded passages can resemble Chika; rounded note-enders can resemble μ's Nico.

signatureSongs.label: The Ondo style suits her — “tabeyou” 1:52 is deep, powerful, and clear, unexpected for a moe singer.
Just two seconds, but argued her strongest solo — clearer and stronger than ever.

notes.text: A reeled-in version of her character voice that proves more technical handle than she's usually credited for.
Surprising vibrato — a powerful held “darou,” one of her most impressive feats ever.
Holds drawn-out notes where childlike voices usually can't — and keeps them flat instead of using vibrato.

6.thesis: The author's runaway favorite voice and arguable best singer in either group — a model of consistency gliding from theatrical depth to crystalline falsetto.
tells: Nasally vowels — especially soft O's that turn nasal in her high range.
A very clear falsetto and a distinct, theatrical deep voice (most exposed in Strawberry Trapper).
Wide vibrato and dynamics for a theatrical quality; vibrato on notes no one else ornaments.
Huge range, recognizable across her whole register.
confusable: vs Mari: hard to split where their nasal textures overlap (Aozora Jumping Heart). vs Hanamaru: Yoshiko's vibrato is wider.

signatureSongs.label: His pick for her strongest performance — strong first notes, then intensity cut via vibrato and dynamics.
The deep, ominous “Yohane, shoukan” bridge — the one song that showcases her lows extensively.

notes.text: Rapid chest → head voice → falsetto in seconds; huge range.
Flawless “angel mode” falsetto and vibrato on “mama.”
His favorite part of her range — deep enough for power yet keeping clarity and control.
Nasally soft O's on “aitai no wa” and a very clear falsetto.

7.thesis: A clear, stiff-and-grand voice and Aqours' great late bloomer — early pitch flaws, but the most dramatically improved singer across the run.
tells: Unmistakable tell: a lisp (audible on “suteki,” Mattete 1:18/1:35).
A stiffness and grandeur that stays even as her technique improves.
Strongly opens and rounds vowels (hard O's “desho no”); keeps hard E's tight and high.
Better at power vocals than mellow ones; owns regal, grand closing sections.

signatureSongs.label: “Finally got love” — a duet with Yoshiko; the single huge improvement that sits above everything on the trajectory.
Owns the most regal closing role — “no one fills that role like she can.”

notes.text: Much cleaner in technicality, power, and pitch; hard E's kept tight and high.
More comfortable and elegant on power lines than mellow ones; easy to pick out by the lisp.
Smooths the contrast between Mari's nasal and Kanan's smoother voice.

8.thesis: The most distinctively-voiced member with the weakest start — a warm, jagged-toned timbre player whose arc is the steepest improvement curve in the group.
tells: Timbre — not technicality or power — is her asset; unmistakable even grouped or autotuned.
Jagged transitions: accelerates rapidly into and out of pitches before settling; sustained notes waver rather than hold flat.
Pronounces soft O's (“ah”) like soft A's (“at”), dragging from a forward to an open mouth.
Ends choruses with a quick faint exhale rather than a spike; a consistently deeper tone.
confusable: vs μ's Nozomi: sings eerily similar — close pitch and nasal placement; often described as a Nozomi/Nico combination.

signatureSongs.label: Her best performance by far — “3:35 ii hi da” is the model Kanan high note: deep, warm, consistent.
Shockingly good (scored 9.5) — only she and Chika did something out-of-the-ordinary.

notes.text: Jagged “omoide” entry; her deeper 4:44 bridge is the best match for her voice.
Most unique voice in the group — a Kanan solo easily creates a change of pace.
Hitches and unorthodox dynamics; warm, enthusiastic background interjections.

9.thesis: The third years' star soloist and the group's theatrical showstopper — a high, nasal voice with a deep reserve of breathy sensuality.
tells: Core tell: nasality at a near-100% hit rate, most noticeable on hard “E” sounds.
The highest voice of Guilty Kiss, but edged so it meshes with the deeper Riko/Yoshiko.
Playful, wacky interjections (the classic Mari “yes”); a theatrical delivery.
A breathy, sensual side; a layered, almost auto-tuned tone at the top of her range.
confusable: vs Yoshiko: overlapping nasal range and texture make Aozora Jumping Heart hard to split.

signatureSongs.label: Perfection — seamlessly weaves folksy and poppy vocals (her seiyuu's min'yo background); the biggest single-song jump in the graphs.
Her sexier, breathy side — sultry bookends with smooth transitions.

notes.text: Nasal near-100%; well-rounded — falsetto, sweeping, power, and the classic Mari “yes.”
The model for done-right nasality; playful interjections.
A layered, auto-tuned high-end texture; plays her voice off the guitar like an instrument.
Nasal stylings amaze — the sweep and vibrato of “kaori wa.”
Her breathy side on full display — great dynamics all the way down to a near-whisper.
